title: Sociology & Social Structures
subtitle: Institutions, stratification & collective life
methodology: METHODOLOGY · Every entry anchors to a landmark study, survey, paper or theoretical text. The hint explains the idea in plain language; the note names the primary publication and flags replication status, revisions and known controversies. Studies and effects carry a replicability badge — 🟢 robust · 🟡 nuanced or reduced · 🔴 failed or severely contested. Map pins mark the institution where the work was carried out.

categories.label: Theories & Models
categories.subtitle: The frameworks that organise the study of institutions and social structure

entries.label: Structural Functionalism
entries.hint: Society is a system of interdependent parts — family, economy, education, religion — each contributing to overall stability, much as organs sustain a body. Parsons formalised this into a general theory of social action; Merton later refined it by distinguishing a practice's manifest (intended, recognised) function from its latent (unintended, often hidden) one, showing institutions can persist for reasons nobody consciously endorses.
entries.note: Parsons, T. (1951). 'The Social System.' Free Press. Merton, R. K. (1949). 'Social Theory and Social Structure.' Free Press, added manifest/latent functions and 'functional alternatives' (the same social need can be met by different institutions). Critiqued from the 1960s onward (by conflict theorists) for treating social stability as the default and struggling to explain conflict, power and rapid social change.

entries.label: Conflict Theory
entries.hint: Society is not a harmonious system but an arena of struggle between groups with opposed material interests — classically, those who own the means of production and those who sell their labour to survive. Marx argued that a society's economic base shapes its politics, law and culture, and that this class conflict is the engine of historical change, not gradual consensus.
entries.note: Marx, K. & Engels, F. (1848). 'Manifesto of the Communist Party.' Elaborated in Marx, K. (1867). 'Das Kapital, Vol. 1.' Verlag von Otto Meisner. Later conflict theorists (Dahrendorf, Collins) generalised the framework beyond economic class to any group locked in a struggle over scarce resources or authority. Critics note that predicted revolutionary outcomes in advanced industrial economies did not materialise as Marx forecast, though the broader emphasis on structural inequality and group conflict remains foundational to sociology.

entries.label: Symbolic Interactionism
entries.hint: Society is built, moment to moment, out of small-scale face-to-face interactions in which people interpret each other's gestures and act toward things based on the meaning those things hold for them — meanings that are negotiated and can shift with context. This 'micro' lens treats the self itself as something continuously constructed through interaction with others, not a fixed inner essence.
entries.note: Mead, G. H. (1934). 'Mind, Self, and Society.' University of Chicago Press (published posthumously from lecture notes by student Charles Morris). Formalised as 'symbolic interactionism' by Blumer, H. (1969). 'Symbolic Interactionism: Perspective and Method.' Prentice-Hall. Extended into dramaturgical analysis by Erving Goffman (st4). Critiqued for focusing so tightly on micro-level interaction that it can lose sight of large-scale structural forces (class, institutions) that constrain those interactions.

entries.label: Social Action & Verstehen
entries.hint: To explain social behaviour, a sociologist must first understand the subjective meaning the actor attaches to their own action (Verstehen, 'interpretive understanding') — not just observe outward behaviour as if it were a physical event. Weber classified action into types (traditional, affectual, value-rational, instrumentally rational) and argued modern society is defined by the rising dominance of instrumentally rational calculation over tradition and emotion.
entries.note: Weber, M. (1922). 'Wirtschaft und Gesellschaft' (Economy and Society), published posthumously by his wife Marianne Weber, Mohr Siebeck. Weber's interpretive method sits in tension with more positivist, statistics-driven sociology (e.g. Durkheim's Suicide, st1) over whether subjective meaning can be studied with the same rigour as external social facts — a methodological divide that still runs through the discipline.

entries.label: Anomie & Strain Theory
entries.loc: Columbia University, New York, New York
entries.hint: Deviance is not primarily caused by individual pathology but by a mismatch between culturally prescribed goals (e.g. wealth) and the legitimate means available to reach them — when society preaches success for everyone but blocks fair access to the ladder, some people innovate through crime, others retreat, others rebel. Durkheim's earlier concept of anomie (normlessness during rapid social change) became Merton's structural theory of why deviance clusters by social position.
entries.note: Durkheim, E. (1893). 'De la division du travail social' introduced anomie; Merton, R. K. (1938). 'Social Structure and Anomie.' American Sociological Review, 3(5), 672–682, built strain theory on top of it. Widely applied and tested through the mid-20th century; later critiqued (by labeling theorists, th6) for assuming a single, shared cultural goal (material success) across all groups, and for not explaining why most people facing the same 'strain' do not turn to crime.

entries.label: Labeling Theory
entries.hint: Deviance is not an inherent property of an act but a status conferred by others' reactions to it — the same behaviour can be ignored, tolerated or severely punished depending on who does it and who is doing the labelling, and once someone is formally labelled 'deviant' (e.g. by arrest), that label itself can push them further into a deviant identity and career.
entries.note: Becker, H. S. (1963). 'Outsiders: Studies in the Sociology of Deviance.' Free Press. Extended by Lemert, E. (1967) into primary vs. secondary deviance. Influential in criminology and the sociology of mental illness (echoing Goffman's total-institution work, st4), though critics note it explains how deviant identities are sustained better than it explains why the initial act occurred in the first place.

entries.label: Bureaucracy & Rational-Legal Authority
entries.hint: Modern organisations are governed not by tradition or a leader's personal charisma but by fixed rules, hierarchical offices and technical competence — a 'rational-legal' form of authority that Weber considered the most efficient way to coordinate large-scale action, but which he also warned could trap individuals in an impersonal 'iron cage' of rules stripped of larger meaning.
entries.note: Weber, M. (1922). 'Wirtschaft und Gesellschaft' (Economy and Society). Mohr Siebeck. The bureaucratic ideal type has proven remarkably durable as a description of large modern organisations (states, corporations, universities), though later organisational sociologists (e.g. Robert Merton's 'bureaucratic ritualism,' Michel Crozier) documented systematic dysfunctions — rule-following that defeats the organisation's actual purpose — that Weber's original model underplayed.

entries.label: Cultural Capital & Social Reproduction
entries.loc: École des Hautes Études en Sciences Sociales, Paris, France
entries.hint: Class advantage is passed down not just through money but through 'cultural capital' — the tastes, manners, vocabulary and knowledge of how institutions work that children absorb at home and that schools reward as if it were pure merit. Bourdieu argued this lets privileged families reproduce their position across generations while the system appears to be a fair, open competition based only on individual ability.
entries.note: Bourdieu, P. (1979). 'La Distinction: Critique sociale du jugement.' Les Éditions de Minuit. Developed earlier with Passeron in 'Les Héritiers' (1964, see st9). The concept has been widely applied and tested in educational sociology across many countries; some scholars (e.g. in later US replications) find the specific 'highbrow culture' markers Bourdieu used in 1960s–70s France travel less well to other national contexts, even as the general reproduction mechanism holds up.

entries.label: Intersectionality
entries.loc: University of Chicago Law School, Chicago, Illinois
entries.hint: Systems of inequality (race, gender, class) do not simply add together — they interact, producing distinct experiences that neither a race-only nor a gender-only lens can capture. Crenshaw showed that Black women could be denied legal remedy in discrimination cases because courts insisted on treating race and sex discrimination as separate claims, structurally erasing harms that only occur at their intersection.
entries.note: Crenshaw, K. (1989). 'Demarginalizing the Intersection of Race and Sex.' University of Chicago Legal Forum, 1989(1), 139–167. Built on earlier Black feminist theorizing (Combahee River Collective, 1977). Now a standard analytic tool across sociology of race, gender and class; some critics argue the framework can be applied so broadly to any combination of identities that it risks losing analytical precision, a live methodological debate within the field itself.

entries.label: Urban Ecology (Chicago School)
entries.hint: Cities grow in predictable concentric rings radiating out from a central business district — a zone of transition (cheap housing, immigrant settlement, high crime) surrounds the core, with progressively wealthier residential zones further out — much as ecological competition sorts species into niches. Park and Burgess mapped Chicago itself to argue urban social patterns follow lawful, near-biological processes of competition for space.
entries.note: Park, R. E. & Burgess, E. W. (1925). 'The City.' University of Chicago Press. The concentric-zone model fit early-20th-century industrial Chicago reasonably well but has been heavily revised since — later urban sociologists (e.g. Harris & Ullman's multiple-nuclei model, 1945) showed many cities, especially those built around the automobile, do not grow in tidy rings at all, so the model is now read as historically specific rather than a universal law of urban form.

entries.label: Social Constructionism
entries.loc: New School for Social Research, New York, New York
entries.hint: Institutions that feel like fixed, objective facts of life — marriage, money, a country's borders, even gender roles — are in fact built and continuously maintained through everyday social agreement and habit, then handed down to new generations as if they had always been natural and inevitable. Berger and Luckmann argued that reality itself, for social purposes, is what a society collectively treats as real.
entries.note: Berger, P. L. & Luckmann, T. (1966). 'The Social Construction of Reality: A Treatise in the Sociology of Knowledge.' Doubleday. Became a foundational text across the sociology of knowledge, institutions and science studies. Critics note that emphasising how thoroughly institutions are 'constructed' can understate how real and materially constraining they feel and function once established — a construction can be just as hard to change as a natural fact.

categories.label: Landmark Studies
categories.subtitle: The defining experiments — and exactly what they found

entries.label: Durkheim's Suicide
entries.hint: Durkheim showed that suicide rates — seemingly the most private, individual of acts — vary systematically and predictably across social categories: Protestants more than Catholics, unmarried more than married, and rates spiking during both economic crashes and economic booms. He argued these patterns reflect differing degrees of social integration and regulation, founding sociology's claim that even individual behaviour is shaped by 'social facts' external to the person.
entries.note: Durkheim, E. (1897). 'Le Suicide: Étude de sociologie.' Félix Alcan, Paris. Douglas, J. D. (1967). 'The Social Meanings of Suicide.' Princeton University Press, challenged the reliability of the official statistics Durkheim relied on, arguing that how a death gets officially classified as 'suicide' is itself a social process shaped by local coroners' biases — meaning Durkheim's patterns may partly reflect differences in record-keeping rather than in actual behaviour. The broad integration/regulation pattern has nonetheless been replicated in many later, more careful studies.

entries.label: Milgram's Small World Experiment
entries.hint: Milgram gave people in Nebraska and Kansas a letter to route, through personal acquaintances only, to a specific stockbroker in Boston they had never met — and the successful chains averaged only about six intermediaries, the origin of the phrase 'six degrees of separation.' The study became the founding empirical claim that social networks, however vast, are startlingly tightly connected.
entries.note: Milgram, S. (1967). 'The Small World Problem.' Psychology Today, 1(1), 61–67. Kleinfeld, J. (2002). 'Could It Be a Big World After All?' Society, 39(2), 61–66, pointed out that in Milgram's original study the vast majority of chains never completed at all (as low as 5% in one version), so the famous 'six' figure came only from a self-selected minority of successful chains. A large-scale email replication (Dodds, Watts & Sabel, 2003, Science, 301, 827–829) found similarly short completion paths but confirmed completion rates remained low, so the network really is 'small' but reaching across it in practice is harder than the popular version of the finding suggests.

entries.label: Whyte's Street Corner Society
entries.hint: Whyte spent three years living in and observing an Italian-American slum neighbourhood in Boston, documenting how a supposedly 'disorganised' immigrant community actually ran on a complex, orderly system of street-corner gangs, informal leadership and mutual obligation — directly contradicting the era's assumption that poor immigrant neighbourhoods were simply chaotic.
entries.note: Whyte, W. F. (1943). 'Street Corner Society: The Social Structure of an Italian Slum.' University of Chicago Press. Boelen, W. A. M. (1992). 'Street Corner Society: Cornerville Revisited.' Journal of Contemporary Ethnography, 21(1), 11–51, revisited the same neighbourhood decades later and disputed several factual details of Whyte's account (including his central informant 'Doc's' real circumstances), arguing Whyte had misread aspects of Italian-American culture — a case frequently cited in methods courses on the risks of relying on a single key informant.

entries.label: Goffman's Asylums
entries.hint: Goffman spent a year as a covert participant-observer inside a large psychiatric hospital and showed how the institution stripped incoming patients of their prior identity through standardised, degrading intake routines, then managed them entirely within the institution's own walls, work, and social life — coining the term 'total institution' for any place (asylums, prisons, monasteries, military boot camps) that controls virtually every aspect of its members' lives.
entries.note: Goffman, E. (1961). 'Asylums: Essays on the Social Situation of Mental Patients and Other Inmates.' Anchor Books. The 'total institution' concept has been applied and broadly confirmed across many other closed institutions studied since (prisons, cults, some care homes), and directly informed the deinstitutionalisation movement in psychiatric care from the 1960s–80s.

entries.label: Wilson's The Truly Disadvantaged
entries.hint: Wilson argued that the deep, persistent poverty concentrated in inner-city Black neighbourhoods was driven primarily by structural economic shifts — the disappearance of manufacturing jobs and the exit of the Black middle class to the suburbs — rather than by a self-perpetuating 'culture of poverty,' reframing a politically charged debate around economic restructuring instead of individual or cultural failure.
entries.note: Wilson, W. J. (1987). 'The Truly Disadvantaged: The Inner City, the Underclass, and Public Policy.' University of Chicago Press. Hugely influential in urban sociology and social policy. Later scholars (surveyed in Small, M. L., Harding, D. J. & Lamont, M. 2010, 'Reconsidering Culture and Poverty,' The ANNALS of the American Academy of Political and Social Science, 629) argued Wilson's structural account had gone too far in dismissing cultural factors entirely, calling for a more integrated structure-plus-culture explanation — including revisions Wilson himself later incorporated.

entries.label: Putnam's Bowling Alone
entries.hint: Putnam documented a decades-long decline in American civic life — falling membership in clubs, unions, PTAs and even bowling leagues — and argued this erosion of 'social capital' (the networks and trust that let communities solve collective problems) weakened democracy itself, not just social life.
entries.note: Putnam, R. D. (2000). 'Bowling Alone: The Collapse and Revival of American Community.' Simon & Schuster. Extremely widely cited, but critics argued Putnam's survey measures conflated several distinct things under 'social capital,' and that some forms of civic connection (online communities, informal networks) were growing even as the specific mid-century associations he tracked declined — suggesting a shift in the form of civic life rather than a simple, uniform collapse.

entries.label: Kohn's Class and Conformity
entries.loc: National Institute of Mental Health, Bethesda, Maryland
entries.hint: Kohn found that a parent's occupation predicted the values they instilled in their children: parents in jobs requiring self-direction and independent judgment raised children to value autonomy and self-reliance, while parents in closely supervised, routinised jobs raised children to value obedience and conformity — showing how a family's position in the economy shapes the psychology it passes down.
entries.note: Kohn, M. L. (1969). 'Class and Conformity: A Study in Values.' Dorsey Press. Kohn, M. L. & Schooler, C. (1983). 'Work and Personality: An Inquiry into the Impact of Social Stratification.' Ablex, extended the finding into a full causal model. Cross-nationally replicated in Kohn's later comparative studies (Poland, Japan, Italy, the 1980s–90s), which found the same occupational self-direction/parental-values link held across very different economic and political systems.

entries.label: Pager's Mark of a Criminal Record
entries.hint: Pager sent matched pairs of fake job applicants with identical resumes — varying only race and whether the applicant listed a criminal record — to real employers, and found a criminal record cut callbacks roughly in half, with the penalty for a Black applicant with a record larger than for an equivalent white applicant, whose 'clean' record advantage nearly erased the racial gap altogether.
entries.note: Pager, D. (2003). 'The Mark of a Criminal Record.' American Journal of Sociology, 108(5), 937–975. Extended by Pager, D., Western, B. & Bonikowski, B. (2009). 'Discrimination in a Low-Wage Labor Market: A Field Experiment.' American Sociological Review, 74(5), 777–799, in New York City. Subsequent meta-analyses of correspondence and audit studies across many countries have consistently found comparable racial discrimination effects in hiring, making this among the most robust, repeatedly confirmed findings in the sociology of stratification and discrimination.

entries.label: Bourdieu & Passeron's The Inheritors
entries.hint: Bourdieu and Passeron showed that French university students from privileged families succeeded not simply because they were smarter or worked harder, but because they arrived already fluent in the tastes, references and unspoken norms the education system rewarded — a fluency their working-class classmates had to learn from scratch, if they got the chance at all.
entries.note: Bourdieu, P. & Passeron, J.-C. (1964). 'Les Héritiers: Les étudiants et la culture.' Les Éditions de Minuit (English translation: 'The Inheritors,' University of Chicago Press, 1979). The cultural-capital mechanism connecting family background to educational success has been widely replicated in later studies across many national education systems, though the specific 'highbrow culture' markers Bourdieu used are more culturally and historically specific to 1960s France.

entries.label: Christakis & Fowler's Social Contagion of Obesity
entries.hint: Analysing 32 years of data from the Framingham Heart Study's dense social network, Christakis and Fowler found that a person's chance of becoming obese rose sharply if a friend became obese — even a friend living far away — and argued this showed obesity spreading through social ties like a socially transmitted behaviour, not just shared environment or genes.
entries.note: Christakis, N. A. & Fowler, J. H. (2007). 'The Spread of Obesity in a Large Social Network over 32 Years.' New England Journal of Medicine, 357(4), 370–379. Shalizi, C. R. & Thomas, A. C. (2011). 'Homophily and Contagion Are Generically Confounded in Observational Social Network Studies.' Sociological Methods & Research, 40(2), 211–239, showed the statistical method used could not actually distinguish genuine social contagion from homophily (friends resembling each other because similar people become friends) or shared environment — the underlying clustering pattern in the data is real, but the strong causal 'contagion' interpretation is seriously disputed.

categories.label: Effects & Phenomena
categories.subtitle: Named, replicable patterns in social and institutional behaviour

entries.label: Thomas Theorem & Self-Fulfilling Prophecy
entries.loc: Columbia University, New York, New York
entries.hint: If people define a situation as real, it becomes real in its consequences, regardless of whether the original definition was objectively true — a rumour that a bank is about to fail can, purely through people's reaction to the rumour, actually cause the bank to fail. Merton later formalised this into the 'self-fulfilling prophecy': a false belief that, once acted upon, brings about the very outcome it predicted.
entries.note: Thomas, W. I. & Thomas, D. S. (1928). 'The Child in America: Behavior Problems and Programs.' Alfred A. Knopf, p. 572, stated the original theorem. Merton, R. K. (1948). 'The Self-Fulfilling Prophecy.' The Antioch Review, 8(2), 193–210, generalised it, connecting it to bank runs and, later, the Rosenthal-Jacobson classroom-expectation studies. The mechanism is well replicated across finance, education and health, though its size varies considerably by context.

entries.label: Matthew Effect (Cumulative Advantage)
entries.loc: Columbia University, New York, New York
entries.hint: Small initial advantages compound over time into large ones — a slightly-better-known scientist gets disproportionately more credit for a co-discovery, a slightly-ahead student gets more teacher attention and pulls further ahead — named after the biblical verse: 'For unto every one that hath shall be given... but from him that hath not shall be taken away.'
entries.note: Merton, R. K. (1968). 'The Matthew Effect in Science.' Science, 159(3810), 56–63, documented it first among scientists' reputations and citation credit. Since replicated across education (early reading ability predicting later gains), economics (wealth begetting wealth) and many other domains — one of the most consistently confirmed patterns in the sociology of stratification.

entries.label: Conspicuous Consumption
entries.hint: People at the top of the economic ladder often signal their status not through practical goods but through visibly wasteful spending — clothes, leisure, elaborate parties — precisely because the impracticality of the expense proves they can afford not to worry about cost, making waste itself the status marker.
entries.note: Veblen, T. (1899). 'The Theory of the Leisure Class: An Economic Study of Institutions.' Macmillan. The core pattern — visible waste as status signal — has been repeatedly confirmed in later consumer research, though the specific goods used to signal status shift by era and culture, from Veblen's carriages and dress to modern luxury brands and vacations.

entries.label: Educational Assortative Mating
entries.loc: University of California, Los Angeles, California
entries.hint: People increasingly marry partners with a similar level of education to their own, and this tendency has grown stronger since the mid-20th century as more women pursued higher education — pairing two high earners (or two low earners) together in the same household, a shift that itself has driven a meaningful share of the rise in household income inequality.
entries.note: Schwartz, C. R. & Mare, R. D. (2005). 'Trends in Educational Assortative Marriage from 1940 to 2003.' Demography, 42(4), 621–646. The rising-homogamy trend and its contribution to household income inequality has been replicated in subsequent studies across multiple countries, making it one of the more robust findings linking family formation patterns to macro-level inequality.

entries.label: White Flight
entries.hint: As Black and other minority families moved into previously all-white urban neighbourhoods in the mid-20th-century United States, white residents frequently responded by moving out to the suburbs in large numbers — accelerating neighbourhood racial turnover and helping to concentrate poverty and disinvestment in the neighbourhoods left behind.
entries.note: Documented across many U.S. cities; formally modelled in Schelling, T. C. (1971). 'Dynamic Models of Segregation.' Journal of Mathematical Sociology, 1(2), 143–186, which showed even a mild individual preference for same-race neighbours could produce near-total segregation through this kind of aggregate movement. Widely confirmed through U.S. Census tract data across the 1950s–1970s, though its pace has slowed considerably since fair-housing enforcement strengthened from the late 1970s onward.

entries.label: Digital Divide
entries.hint: Access to computers, high-speed internet and digital skills is not evenly distributed — it tracks existing lines of income, race, geography and age, so that the same technology that promises to level opportunity can instead widen the very inequalities it was expected to close.
entries.note: Named and tracked in early U.S. National Telecommunications and Information Administration reports (from 1995) and analysed sociologically in DiMaggio, P., Hargittai, E., Celeste, C. & Shafer, S. (2004). 'From Unequal Access to Differentiated Use,' in Social Inequality, ed. K. Neckerman, Russell Sage Foundation. Raw access gaps narrowed substantially as devices became cheaper, but researchers found the divide persisted in a different form — a 'second-level' gap in skills, quality of use and outcomes — confirming the phenomenon while its specific shape shifted.

entries.label: Iron Law of Oligarchy
entries.hint: Even organisations explicitly founded on democratic, egalitarian principles — political parties, labour unions — tend over time to concentrate real decision-making power in the hands of a small, entrenched leadership, because running a large organisation requires specialised skills, full-time attention and control of information that most ordinary members don't have the time or resources to match.
entries.note: Michels, R. (1911). 'Zur Soziologie des Parteiwesens in der modernen Demokratie' (Political Parties). Werner Klinkhardt, based on his study of European socialist parties and trade unions. Later case studies (Lipset, S. M., Trow, M. & Coleman, J. 1956, 'Union Democracy,' Free Press, on the International Typographical Union) found real exceptions — some unions maintained genuine internal democracy for decades — showing the 'law' describes a strong tendency rather than an absolute rule.

entries.label: Bureaucratic Ritualism
entries.loc: Columbia University, New York, New York
entries.hint: Some bureaucrats and employees come to follow official rules and procedures so rigidly that they lose sight of the organisation's actual goals — clinging to the paperwork and the process as an end in itself, even when doing so actively defeats the purpose the rules were originally designed to serve.
entries.note: Merton, R. K. (1940). 'Bureaucratic Structure and Personality.' Social Forces, 18(4), 560–568, identified ritualism as one of Merton's five individual adaptations to the strain between cultural goals and institutional means (alongside th5's innovation, retreatism, rebellion and conformity). Repeatedly documented in studies of large public and private bureaucracies since, though how much reflects individual personality versus a specific organisation's incentive structure remains debated.

entries.label: Residential Hypersegregation
entries.hint: Some U.S. metropolitan areas show Black-white residential segregation so extreme and multidimensional — high on several separate measures of separation at once — that researchers coined a distinct term, 'hypersegregation,' since ordinary single-index segregation measures understated just how thoroughly separated daily life had become in these cities.
entries.note: Massey, D. S. & Denton, N. A. (1989). 'Hypersegregation in U.S. Metropolitan Areas.' Demography, 26(3), 373–391, and 'American Apartheid' (1993), Harvard University Press. The hypersegregation pattern in the originally identified metro areas has been repeatedly confirmed in later Census-based studies through the 2000s–2010s, with slow declines in some cities and persistence in others.

categories.label: Key Concepts
categories.subtitle: The building blocks and vocabulary every student must command

entries.label: Social Facts
entries.hint: Durkheim argued sociology has its own proper subject matter distinct from psychology or biology: 'social facts' — ways of acting, thinking and feeling that exist outside any single individual, are shared collectively, and exert a real, coercive constraint on behaviour (laws, currency, language, religious rites) — and that these should be studied as things, using evidence, not reasoned about from an armchair.
entries.note: Durkheim, E. (1895). 'Les Règles de la méthode sociologique' (The Rules of Sociological Method). Félix Alcan, Paris. The concept established sociology's claim to be an independent science with its own object of study, distinct from psychology's focus on the individual mind — a foundational methodological move for the discipline.

entries.label: Status vs Role
entries.loc: Columbia University, New York, New York
entries.hint: A 'status' is a recognised social position a person occupies (mother, judge, student), while a 'role' is the set of expected behaviours that go along with that position — the same person holds many statuses simultaneously, and role conflict arises when the behavioural expectations attached to two of their statuses pull in opposite directions at once.
entries.note: Linton, R. (1936). 'The Study of Man: An Introduction.' D. Appleton-Century Company, introduced the status/role distinction as basic sociological vocabulary. Later refined by Merton, R. K. (1957) into 'role sets' — the observation that even a single status (e.g. 'doctor') comes with multiple, sometimes conflicting roles attached to different other people.

entries.label: Life Chances
entries.hint: A person's objective probability of obtaining the good things in life — health, education, income, life expectancy — shaped systematically by the social class, and specifically the market position, into which they are born, rather than purely by individual effort or talent.
entries.note: Weber, M. (1922). 'Wirtschaft und Gesellschaft' (Economy and Society). Mohr Siebeck, developed 'life chances' (Lebenschancen) as an alternative to Marx's narrower focus on ownership of the means of production, arguing market position (skills, credentials, access to credit) shapes life outcomes even among people who are neither capitalists nor workers in the classical Marxist sense.

entries.label: Social Capital
entries.hint: Beyond financial or human capital, people and communities also draw on the value embedded in their social networks and relationships of trust — connections that make it easier to find a job, get help in a crisis, or organise collective action — and communities with dense, trusting networks tend to solve shared problems more effectively than equally wealthy but socially fragmented ones.
entries.note: Developed by Bourdieu, P. (1986). 'The Forms of Capital,' in Handbook of Theory and Research for the Sociology of Education, ed. J. Richardson, Greenwood, and independently by Coleman, J. S. (1988). 'Social Capital in the Creation of Human Capital.' American Journal of Sociology, 94, S95–S120; popularised at the national level by Putnam (st6). Critics note the concept is used so broadly — sometimes an individual resource, sometimes a community-level property — that it can be hard to measure consistently across studies.

entries.label: Emotional Labor
entries.hint: Many service jobs require workers to manage and display specific emotions as part of the job itself — a flight attendant must appear warmly cheerful no matter how a passenger treats her, a bill collector must project stern authority — and sustaining this managed performance over an entire shift is itself a form of labour, with its own costs of fatigue and self-alienation.
entries.note: Hochschild, A. R. (1983). 'The Managed Heart: Commercialization of Human Feeling.' University of California Press, based on fieldwork with airline flight attendants and debt collectors. Widely extended since to nursing, teaching, retail and many other service occupations, and consistently linked in later research to burnout when required 'surface acting' conflicts too sharply with a worker's genuine feelings.

entries.label: Moral Panic
entries.loc: London School of Economics, London, England
entries.hint: Occasionally a relatively minor or localised incident gets amplified by media coverage and public reaction into a perceived large-scale threat to social values, producing a wave of alarm, and often new laws or policing, wildly disproportionate to the actual scale of the original problem — the deviant group singled out in the process becomes society's 'folk devil' for that moment.
entries.note: Cohen, S. (1972). 'Folk Devils and Moral Panics: The Creation of the Mods and Rockers.' MacGibbon and Kee, based on British media coverage of youth subculture clashes at seaside resorts. Since applied to many other episodes (satanic ritual abuse scares, various drug panics), and remains a standard tool in the sociology of deviance and media for analysing how a threat's perceived scale can diverge sharply from its measured, actual scale.

entries.label: Doing Gender
entries.loc: University of California, Santa Barbara, California
entries.hint: Gender is not simply a fixed trait a person has, but something continually 'done' or accomplished through everyday interaction — how one talks, moves, dresses and responds to others in situationally appropriate, gendered ways, reinforced by the risk of social sanction if one doesn't perform it as expected.
entries.note: West, C. & Zimmerman, D. H. (1987). 'Doing Gender.' Gender & Society, 1(2), 125–151. Reframed gender from a fixed individual attribute into an ongoing interactional accomplishment, shifting analysis toward the situational, everyday work of producing gender difference — highly influential since, though later scholars (Risman, B. 2009) argued the framework needs to better account for structural and institutional gender inequality, not just interactional performance.

entries.label: Institutional Isomorphism
entries.hint: Organisations operating in the same field — hospitals, universities, corporations in the same industry — tend to become more similar to one another over time, not necessarily because similarity makes them more efficient, but because they copy each other's practices, respond to the same regulatory pressures, and hire staff trained in the same professional norms.
entries.note: DiMaggio, P. J. & Powell, W. W. (1983). 'The Iron Cage Revisited: Institutional Isomorphism and Collective Rationality in Organizational Fields.' American Sociological Review, 48(2), 147–160, distinguished three mechanisms: coercive (regulation), mimetic (copying under uncertainty) and normative (shared professional training). Widely applied and confirmed across many organisational fields since, becoming a central concept of neo-institutional organisational sociology.

entries.label: Secularization
entries.loc: New School for Social Research, New York, New York
entries.hint: As societies modernise — industrialise, urbanise, expand mass education — religious institutions are theorised to lose social influence and authority over public life, private belief, and everyday behaviour, with religion becoming one privatised option among many rather than the shared framework organising the whole society.
entries.note: Classically formulated in Berger, P. L. (1967). 'The Sacred Canopy: Elements of a Sociological Theory of Religion.' Doubleday, building on Weber's earlier account of rationalisation. Berger himself later published a striking retraction, 'The Desecularization of the World' (1999), Eerdmans, pointing to continued and even rising religiosity across much of the world outside Western Europe — a case where a confident mid-century sociological prediction had to be substantially revised.

categories.label: Methods & Approaches
categories.subtitle: The tools and paradigms that generate the field's evidence

entries.label: Sample Survey Method
entries.loc: Columbia University, New York, New York
entries.hint: Rather than studying one community deeply, the sample survey administers standardised questions to a carefully drawn random sample of a much larger population, letting researchers estimate opinions, behaviours or attitudes across an entire country from a few thousand respondents — the workhorse method behind most quantitative sociology since the mid-20th century.
entries.note: Formalised at Columbia's Bureau of Applied Social Research under Paul Lazarsfeld, e.g. Lazarsfeld, P. F., Berelson, B. & Gaudet, H. (1944). 'The People's Choice.' Duell, Sloan and Pearce, one of the first major academic voting-behaviour surveys. Survey quality depends heavily on sampling method and question wording, both extensively studied fields in their own right within methodology.

entries.label: Urban Ethnography / Participant Observation
entries.hint: Borrowed and adapted from anthropology, this method has sociologists live within and directly observe an urban community over an extended period — riding along with street gangs, working alongside factory employees, sitting in on courtroom proceedings — to document social order and meaning that a survey questionnaire alone could never capture.
entries.note: Exemplified by Whyte, W. F. (1943). 'Street Corner Society.' University of Chicago Press (st3), part of the wider Chicago School tradition of first-hand urban fieldwork. Later urban ethnographers (e.g. Elijah Anderson, Mitchell Duneier) have extended the method while grappling with the same methodological questions about a single researcher's access, interpretation and influence on what they observe.

entries.label: Audit / Correspondence Study Method
entries.hint: To test for discrimination without relying on people's self-reports (which rarely admit to bias), researchers send closely matched pairs of fictional applicants — identical except for the one trait being tested, such as race or a criminal record — to real job openings, apartments or loan applications, then compare callback rates.
entries.note: Formalised in sociology by Pager, D. (2003). 'The Mark of a Criminal Record.' American Journal of Sociology, 108(5), 937–975 (st8), building on earlier housing-discrimination audit studies from the 1970s–80s. Now a standard field-experimental method across the sociology of labour markets, housing and discrimination, valued for directly measuring behaviour rather than self-reported attitudes.

entries.label: Social Network Analysis
entries.hint: Rather than treating individuals as isolated units, this method formally maps who is connected to whom — friendship, kinship, communication, collaboration — as a mathematical graph of nodes and ties, letting researchers measure structural properties (how central someone is, how tightly clustered a group is) that shape behaviour independently of any individual's personal traits.
entries.note: Pioneered by Moreno, J. L. (1934). 'Who Shall Survive?' Nervous and Mental Disease Publishing Co., which introduced the sociogram. Formalised mathematically by the Harvard structuralists (Harrison White and students) from the 1970s onward, and later applied at large scale in studies like Milgram's small-world experiment (st2) and Christakis & Fowler's network-contagion research (st10).

entries.label: Content Analysis
entries.hint: To study how media coverage shapes public perception of an issue — say, a supposed youth-crime wave — researchers systematically code a large sample of news articles or broadcasts for recurring themes, framing and word choice, turning impressionistic claims about media bias or amplification into countable, comparable data.
entries.note: Systematised in Berelson, B. (1952). 'Content Analysis in Communication Research.' Free Press. Directly underlies later moral-panic research (Cohen, co6), which relied on systematic coding of British newspaper coverage to demonstrate that media portrayal of the 1964 Mods and Rockers clashes was disproportionate to the actual, much smaller scale of the disturbances.

entries.label: Historical-Comparative Method
entries.hint: Rather than studying one society at one moment, this method systematically compares social institutions (religion, law, economic organisation) across different historical periods and civilisations, looking for how a specific configuration of ideas and material conditions in one case produced outcomes that a different configuration, elsewhere, did not.
entries.note: Exemplified in Weber, M. (1904–05). 'Die protestantische Ethik und der Geist des Kapitalismus' (The Protestant Ethic and the Spirit of Capitalism). Archiv für Sozialwissenschaft und Sozialpolitik, which compared Western European Protestant regions to other religious traditions and regions to argue a specific religious ethic helped enable modern capitalism's emergence in one place and not others. The method remains central to historical and comparative sociology, though it is inherently harder to isolate single causal variables than a controlled experiment.

categories.label: Pioneers & Theorists
categories.subtitle: The researchers who shaped the discipline

entries.label: Émile Durkheim
entries.hint: Durkheim founded sociology as an independent academic discipline, arguing it had its own proper object of study — 'social facts,' patterns that exist above and constrain any single individual — and proved the case empirically by showing even suicide, the most private of acts, followed predictable social patterns.
entries.note: Durkheim, E. (1897). 'Le Suicide' and (1895). 'Les Règles de la méthode sociologique' (st1, co1). Founded the journal L'Année Sociologique (1898) and held the first French professorship explicitly in sociology, at the University of Bordeaux and later the Sorbonne.

entries.label: Max Weber
entries.hint: Weber insisted sociology must grasp the subjective meaning behind social action, not just its outward form, while also tracing how modern society was becoming ever more dominated by rational calculation and impersonal bureaucratic rules — a tension between meaning and rationalisation that runs through nearly all his work.
entries.note: Weber, M. (1922). 'Wirtschaft und Gesellschaft' (Economy and Society), published posthumously (th4, th7). Also authored 'The Protestant Ethic and the Spirit of Capitalism' (1904–05, me6). Along with Durkheim and Marx, considered one of sociology's three classical founders.

entries.label: Karl Marx
entries.hint: Marx argued that a society's economic structure — who owns productive property and who must sell their labour to survive — shapes its politics, law and ideas, and that the resulting conflict between classes, not gradual consensus, is the true engine of historical change.
entries.note: Marx, K. (1867). 'Das Kapital, Vol. 1.' Verlag von Otto Meisner; co-authored with Engels, F. (1848). 'Manifesto of the Communist Party' (th2). Though writing primarily as a political economist and philosopher rather than an academic sociologist, Marx's class-conflict framework became one of the discipline's foundational paradigms.

entries.label: Talcott Parsons
entries.hint: Parsons built the most ambitious general theory in American sociology's history, attempting to specify the functional requirements every society must meet to survive and reproduce itself, and dominated the discipline's theoretical mainstream through the 1950s–60s before conflict theorists mounted a sustained challenge.
entries.note: Parsons, T. (1951). 'The Social System.' Free Press (th1). Chaired Harvard's Department of Social Relations, training a generation of American sociologists; his structural-functionalist synthesis was later criticised for being too static and consensus-oriented to account for conflict and rapid social change.

entries.label: Robert K. Merton
entries.loc: Columbia University, New York, New York
entries.hint: Merton took Parsons's grand functionalist theory and made it empirically usable — distinguishing a practice's intended function from its hidden one, showing how blocked opportunity produces deviance, and naming recurring social patterns (the Matthew effect, bureaucratic ritualism, self-fulfilling prophecies) so precisely that his own terms entered everyday language.
entries.note: Merton, R. K. (1949). 'Social Theory and Social Structure.' Free Press, collecting his most influential essays (th5, ef2, ef8). Long based at Columbia University's sociology department alongside Paul Lazarsfeld, pairing Merton's theory with Lazarsfeld's survey methodology into one of the discipline's most productive mid-century partnerships.

entries.label: George Herbert Mead
entries.hint: Mead argued the self is not something we are born with but something built up through social interaction, as children learn to see themselves from the perspective of others — laying the philosophical groundwork for symbolic interactionism's entire micro-sociological tradition, though he never used that later term himself.
entries.note: Mead, G. H. (1934). 'Mind, Self, and Society.' University of Chicago Press, assembled posthumously from student lecture notes by Charles Morris (th3). A philosopher by training in Chicago's pragmatist tradition, Mead's ideas were formalised into sociology proper by his student Herbert Blumer.

entries.label: Erving Goffman
entries.hint: Goffman treated everyday social life as a kind of theatre, in which people continuously manage the impression they give others through performance, costume and setting — and then showed the darker flip side of that same idea inside a psychiatric hospital, where an institution can strip a person of the very self they were trying to present.
entries.note: Goffman, E. (1959). 'The Presentation of Self in Everyday Life.' University of Edinburgh Social Sciences Research Centre (later Doubleday); also 'Asylums' (1961) (st4). One of the most cited sociologists of the 20th century, bridging symbolic interactionism and a distinctly literary, closely observed style of sociological writing.

entries.label: Pierre Bourdieu
entries.loc: École des Hautes Études en Sciences Sociales, Paris, France
entries.hint: Bourdieu showed that class advantage reproduces itself not just through money but through embodied taste, manners and unspoken cultural know-how that schools reward as though it were pure natural talent — building an influential toolkit (habitus, cultural capital, symbolic capital) for tracing how inequality gets absorbed into individual bodies and dispositions.
entries.note: Bourdieu, P. (1979). 'La Distinction.' Les Éditions de Minuit (th8); with Passeron, 'Les Héritiers' (1964). One of the most influential sociologists of the late 20th century across education, culture and class research worldwide, though some of his specific French cultural markers travel less well to other national contexts.

entries.label: W. E. B. Du Bois
entries.hint: Years before the Chicago School became famous for urban sociology, Du Bois conducted a rigorous door-to-door survey of a Black Philadelphia neighbourhood, using original empirical methods to counter racist theories then dominant in American social science, and later developed the concept of 'double consciousness' to describe the psychological toll of living under a racial system built to devalue you.
entries.note: Du Bois, W. E. B. (1899). 'The Philadelphia Negro: A Social Study.' University of Pennsylvania Press — widely considered the first major empirical community study in American sociology, predating comparable Chicago School urban ethnographies by decades. Also authored 'The Souls of Black Folk' (1903). Long marginalised within the discipline's own mainstream canon despite this methodological priority, a fact sociologists of the discipline's history now actively work to correct.

entries.label: Harriet Martineau
entries.hint: Often called the first female sociologist, Martineau translated and condensed Auguste Comte's founding positivist texts into English, making them accessible to a far wider audience than the French originals, while conducting her own systematic comparative observations of everyday American social life and writing one of the first methodological treatises on how to observe a society's morals and institutions rigorously.
entries.note: Martineau, H. (1838). 'How to Observe Morals and Manners.' Charles Knight and Co. — an early, systematic methodological guide to comparative social observation, alongside her 'Society in America' (1837), Saunders and Otley. Long overlooked in the discipline's standard founding narrative, which centred almost exclusively on Comte, Marx, Durkheim and Weber, despite her earlier methodological contributions.

entries.label: Patricia Hill Collins
entries.hint: Collins developed 'Black feminist thought' and standpoint theory within sociology itself, arguing that Black women's distinctive social position gives rise to forms of knowledge systematically excluded from mainstream sociology, and that race, gender and class must be analysed together as an interlocking system rather than as separate, additive categories.
entries.note: Collins, P. H. (1990). 'Black Feminist Thought: Knowledge, Consciousness, and the Politics of Empowerment.' Unwin Hyman (th9, co7). Provided the specifically sociological articulation of intersectional standpoint theory that complements Kimberlé Crenshaw's parallel legal-scholarship formulation of intersectionality.

categories.label: Debates & Open Questions
categories.subtitle: Live controversies — where the field is actively contesting the evidence

entries.label: Structural Functionalism vs Conflict Theory
entries.hint: Parsons's functionalism treated society as fundamentally a stable, self-equilibrating system held together by shared values — but critics argued this made conflict, coercion and rapid social change look like pathological exceptions rather than the normal, constant condition Marx and later conflict theorists took them to be.
entries.note: Parsons, T. (1951, th1) laid out the functionalist consensus model; Dahrendorf, R. (1959). 'Class and Class Conflict in Industrial Society.' Stanford University Press, delivered one of the sharpest formal critiques, arguing every society is held together by coercion of some by others as much as by shared value consensus. Neither paradigm fully displaced the other — most working sociologists today draw selectively on both rather than treating the mid-century 'paradigm war' as resolved in either direction.

entries.label: Is Sociology a Science? Positivism vs Interpretivism
entries.hint: Durkheim insisted social facts should be studied externally and objectively, 'as things,' the way natural scientists study physical phenomena. Weber countered that social action can only be properly explained by first grasping the subjective meaning the actor attaches to it — a fundamentally interpretive task no purely external measurement can substitute for.
entries.note: Durkheim, E. (1895, co1) laid out the positivist programme; Weber, M. (1904). 'Die Objektivität sozialwissenschaftlicher und sozialpolitischer Erkenntnis.' Archiv für Sozialwissenschaft und Sozialpolitik, argued for interpretive Verstehen as methodologically necessary. Sociology has largely avoided a public rupture on this question but contains the same underlying methodological split between quantitative-positivist and qualitative-interpretive wings to this day.

entries.label: Milgram's Small World: How Small Is It, Really?
entries.loc: University of Alaska Fairbanks, Fairbanks, Alaska
entries.hint: The famous claim that any two strangers are connected by only about six intermediaries rests on a surprisingly shaky foundation: in Milgram's original study, the overwhelming majority of letter chains never reached their target at all, so the celebrated 'six degrees' figure came from only the small, self-selected fraction of chains that happened to succeed.
entries.note: Kleinfeld, J. (2002). 'Could It Be a Big World After All? The 'Six Degrees of Separation' Myth.' Society, 39(2), 61–66, reviewed Milgram's original data and found completion rates as low as 5% in some replications. Dodds, P. S., Muhamad, R. & Watts, D. J. (2003). 'An Experimental Study of Search in Global Social Networks.' Science, 301(5634), 827–829, ran a large modern email-chain replication and found similarly short completed paths but confirmed low overall completion rates.

entries.label: Does Obesity Really Spread Through Social Networks?
entries.hint: Christakis and Fowler's finding that friends' obesity predicted a person's own future obesity was widely read as evidence of social contagion — until statisticians showed the same observational data pattern would look identical whether the cause was genuine contagion, homophily, or simply sharing the same environment, meaning the original study alone could not distinguish between these very different explanations.
entries.note: Christakis, N. A. & Fowler, J. H. (2007, st10) proposed the contagion interpretation. Shalizi, C. R. & Thomas, A. C. (2011). 'Homophily and Contagion Are Generically Confounded in Observational Social Network Studies.' Sociological Methods & Research, 40(2), 211–239, showed mathematically that standard statistical models used in this research cannot separate the three explanations without additional data, a critique that reshaped methodological standards across social network epidemiology.

entries.label: Structure vs Culture in Explaining Urban Poverty
entries.hint: For decades, invoking 'culture' to explain persistent Black urban poverty was seen as dangerously close to blaming the poor for their own condition, pushing most sociologists toward purely structural explanations — until a new generation of scholars argued abandoning culture entirely left real, observable local norms unexamined, and called for a more integrated account combining both.
entries.note: Wilson, W. J. (1987, st5) shifted the field decisively toward structural explanation, partly against earlier, cruder 'culture of poverty' arguments (Lewis, O. 1966). Small, M. L., Harding, D. J. & Lamont, M. (2010). 'Reconsidering Culture and Poverty.' The ANNALS of the American Academy of Political and Social Science, 629(1), 6–27, argued for reintegrating culture as one factor among several, carefully distinguished from older, more stigmatizing formulations.

entries.label: Is the Secularization Thesis Wrong?
entries.hint: Mid-20th-century sociology confidently predicted modernization would steadily erode religion's social authority everywhere — until continued and even rising religious vitality across much of the world outside Western Europe led one of the theory's own most prominent architects to publicly reverse his position, arguing secularization was, at most, a regional pattern rather than a universal law of modernity.
entries.note: Berger, P. L. (1967, co9) formulated the classical secularization thesis. Berger, P. L. (ed.) (1999). 'The Desecularization of the World.' Eerdmans, retracted its universal claim. Rational-choice 'religious economies' theorists (Stark, R. & Finke, R. 2000, 'Acts of Faith,' University of California Press) went further, arguing secularization was never a coherent, well-evidenced theory even for the West.

entries.label: The Coleman Report: Do Schools or Families Drive Achievement Gaps?
entries.hint: A massive federally commissioned survey set out to measure how much school funding and resources explained gaps in student achievement — and found, to widespread surprise, that family and peer background explained far more of the variation than differences in school spending or facilities did, a finding that shaped decades of school desegregation and funding policy debates.
entries.note: Coleman, J. S. et al. (1966). 'Equality of Educational Opportunity.' U.S. Department of Health, Education, and Welfare (the 'Coleman Report'). Later research using more sophisticated methods (e.g. Chetty, R., Friedman, J. N. & Rockoff, J. E. 2014, 'Measuring the Impacts of Teachers,' American Economic Review) found individual teacher quality does have substantial, measurable long-run effects, partially qualifying Coleman's resource-doesn't-matter-much conclusion without overturning his core finding that family background remains the largest factor.

entries.label: Is Bureaucracy Actually Efficient?
entries.hint: Weber presented rational-legal bureaucracy as the most efficient possible way to organize large-scale collective action — but empirical studies of real bureaucracies kept finding the opposite in practice: rules meant to ensure fairness instead produced rigid rule-following that defeated the organization's own goals, and self-perpetuating dysfunction remarkably hard to fix.
entries.note: Weber, M. (1922, th7) presented the rational-legal ideal type. Merton, R. K. (1940, ef8) identified bureaucratic ritualism as a systematic byproduct. Crozier, M. (1963). 'Le Phénomène bureaucratique.' Éditions du Seuil, documented self-reinforcing 'bureaucratic vicious circles' in French organizations that resisted reform precisely because of the same rule-bound structure meant to make them efficient.

categories.label: Applications
categories.subtitle: Where the science leaves the lab

entries.label: Ban the Box & Audit-Study-Informed Policy
entries.hint: Audit studies showing large, measurable hiring penalties for applicants with criminal records directly informed a policy movement to remove the criminal-history checkbox from initial job applications, delaying that disclosure until later in the hiring process so an applicant's other qualifications get a fair first look.
entries.note: The 'Ban the Box' campaign, launched by the formerly incarcerated advocacy group All of Us or None in 2004, drew directly on audit-study evidence like Pager's (me3, st8) to argue for delaying criminal-history disclosure in hiring. Over 35 U.S. states have since adopted some form of the policy; evaluation evidence on its actual effect has been genuinely mixed, with some studies finding it can increase statistical discrimination against Black applicants generally once individual records are no longer visible early.

entries.label: Civic Engagement & Social Capital Policy Initiatives
entries.hint: Concern over declining civic participation directly inspired practical programs — encouraging local associational membership, structured neighborhood gathering initiatives, and policy experiments — aimed at deliberately rebuilding the kind of dense, trusting community networks that make collective problem-solving possible.
entries.note: Putnam, R. D.'s 'Bowling Alone' (2000, st6, co4) directly launched the Saguaro Seminar at Harvard's Kennedy School, a practitioner-academic initiative producing concrete civic-engagement toolkits adopted by numerous U.S. city governments and nonprofits. Effectiveness of these interventions in measurably rebuilding social capital at scale remains an active, contested area of program evaluation research.

entries.label: Corporate Culture & Institutional Isomorphism in HR Practice
entries.hint: Organizational sociology's finding that companies in the same industry converge on similar practices — not necessarily because those practices are objectively best, but through mimicry, regulatory pressure and shared professional norms — is now directly used by management consultants and HR departments to explain why certain diversity programs, governance structures or management fads spread rapidly across an industry.
entries.note: DiMaggio, P. J. & Powell, W. W.'s institutional isomorphism framework (1983, co8) has been directly applied in management and organizational-behavior research and is now standard material in business-school organizational theory courses, informing how consultants explain and predict which management practices will spread through an industry regardless of measured effectiveness.

entries.label: School Desegregation & Funding Policy
entries.hint: Findings that family and peer background, more than school facilities alone, drove achievement gaps became a central argument for school desegregation busing policy — the reasoning being that if concentrated disadvantage itself hurt outcomes, integrating schools across those concentrations, not just equalizing per-pupil spending, was necessary to close the gap.
entries.note: The Coleman Report's (1966, db7) findings were directly cited in the U.S. Supreme Court's 'Swann v. Charlotte-Mecklenburg Board of Education' (1971) decision upholding busing as a desegregation remedy. Busing programs produced measurable short- and medium-term achievement gains in numerous later evaluations, but also accelerated white flight (ef5) in several cities, illustrating how a single body of research can inform policy with genuinely mixed downstream social effects.

entries.label: Restorative Justice Programs
entries.hint: If formally labeling someone a criminal tends to lock them further into a deviant identity and career, then diversion programs that instead bring offenders and victims together to negotiate direct restitution and accountability — outside the formal court and prison system — should, in principle, reduce the very reoffending that harsh formal processing was supposed to prevent.
entries.note: The 1974 Elmira, Ontario case, in which two young offenders met their vandalism victims directly and negotiated restitution, is widely cited as launching the modern restorative-justice movement, drawing on labeling theory's (Becker, th6) logic that formal processing itself can worsen outcomes. Evaluation evidence generally shows modest but real reductions in reoffending and higher victim satisfaction compared to conventional court processing, though effect sizes vary by program design and offense type.

entries.label: Consumer Marketing & Status-Signaling Research
entries.loc: Madison Avenue, New York, New York
entries.hint: Veblen's century-old observation that people buy visibly wasteful goods specifically to signal status became one of the advertising and luxury-goods industry's most directly applied pieces of sociological theory — brand positioning, scarcity marketing and 'aspirational' advertising all consciously exploit the conspicuous-consumption mechanism Veblen first named as a social critique.
entries.note: Veblen, T.'s 'conspicuous consumption' concept (1899, ef3) has been explicitly incorporated into marketing and consumer-research literature since the mid-20th century, including studies of 'Veblen goods,' whose demand paradoxically rises with price. The luxury goods industry directly designs pricing and scarcity strategies around the signaling mechanism Veblen described, turning a critique of the leisure class into a working commercial playbook.
